Tries
(cid:73) Information retrieval
(cid:73) A data structure used to work with strings that beneﬁt from their
structural properties
(cid:73) The memory space required to represent a dictionary is reduced: the
common root is represented only once
(cid:73) Economy of memory when there are many common preﬁxes
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
3 / 28

Tries
(cid:73) A data structure based on the digital representation of the elements
(cid:73) A tree with a root k-th ordered, where k is the number of digits
(letters of an alphabet)
(cid:73) It is assumed that the elements are represented by sequences of digits
(letters) of the same length m (|U| = mk )
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
4 / 28

Tries - the data structure
(cid:73) A linked list of nodes where each node has k children
(cid:73) Suppose the alphabet {0, . . . , k − 1}; the elements from S are keys,
and the boundary nodes stores informations associated to these keys
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
5 / 28

Tries
A trie that stores a collection of words S, |S| = n from a k-sized alphabet
has the following properties:
(cid:73) any internal node has at most k children
(cid:73) the tree has n external nodes
(cid:73) the height of the tree is equal to the length of the longest word in S
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
6 / 28

Tries - The search operation
(cid:73) Search for an element a in the structure t: follow the path described
by the sequence a[0], . . . a[m − 1]
Function search(a, m, t)
begin
i ← 0
p ← t
while (p (cid:54)= NULL AND i < m) do
p ← p → succ[a[i]]
i ← i + 1
return p
end
(cid:73) The worst-case time complexity: O(m)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
7 / 28

Tries - insertion
(cid:73) Inserting a word x in the structure t: it follows the path described by
the sequence x[0], . . . x[m − 1]; add a new node for the components
for which there are no nodes in t
Figure: The insertion of key 110
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
8 / 28

Tries - deletion
(cid:73) An element x to be removed is divided into:
(cid:73) a common preﬁx
(cid:73) a suﬃx that no longer belongs to any element
(cid:73) Go through the path described by x and store the nodes in a stack
(cid:73) Go back through this path and if all successors are nil, then remove
the node
Figure: The deletion of key 102
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
9 / 28

Compacting the chains
(cid:73) A rare structure stores few keys; there are many chains of
intermediate nodes with only one successor
(cid:73) Removing these chains would lead to an improved space and time
complexity; keep only the intermediate nodes with at least 2
successors
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
11 / 28

Compacting the chains
(cid:73) The compact structure stores:
(cid:73) in the internal nodes, the position from which the keys with a common
preﬁx diﬀer
(cid:73) in the boundary nodes, the key.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
12 / 28

Compacting the chains
A digital compacted trie which keeps a collection of words S, |S| = n,
from an alphabet of size k has the following properties:
(cid:73) each internal node has at least 2 children and at most k children
(cid:73) the trie has n external nodes
(cid:73) the number of nodes is O(n)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
13 / 28

Compacting the chains - Operations
(cid:73) The search operation: similarly with the uncompacted case, except
that for each node the value from the stored position of the node is
tested, and when it reaches a boundary node the key is tested
(cid:73) The insert operation involves a search of the key, followed by the
insertion of a new node to distinguish by the position on which the
last, respectively the new node diﬀers
Figure: The insertion of the key 0111
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
14 / 28

Compacting the chains - Operations
(cid:73) The delete operation: in a similar manner to the uncompacted case;
(cid:73) search for the node that stores the key and store the path in a stack;
(cid:73) remove the node with that key
(cid:73) go back through this path and remove the nodes with one successor
Figure: The deletion of the key 1011
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
15 / 28

Patricia structures
(cid:73) Practical Algorithm to Retrieve Information Coded in Alphanumeric
(cid:73) A digital binary tree is a trie over the alphabet {0, 1}.
(cid:73) The number of the boundary nodes is equal with the number of
internal nodes plus one.
(cid:73) If we add an extra internal node, we could store the keys in the
internal nodes. The new node will be the root and will have one (left)
child. The position from the root will be -1.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
17 / 28

Patricia structures - Example
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
18 / 28

Patricia structures - Operations
(cid:73) The search operation - may end when the current position is
smaller then the last tested position
(cid:73) The insert operation: search for the key in the structure, identify
the ﬁrst position j on which x and the key diﬀers;
Figure: The insertion of the key 1100
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
19 / 28

Patricia structures - Operations
(cid:73) The delete operation - example
Figure: The deletion of the key 0111
Theorem:
Suppose that we create a Patricia structure starting from an empty
structure by n insertions of keys randomly generated. Then the search
operation requires O(log n) comparisons on average.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
20 / 28

Tries - Applications
(cid:73) Find all keys starting with a given preﬁx (the autocomplete function)
(cid:73) Find the longest key that is a preﬁx of query string (example: to send
packets, choose the IP address that is the longest preﬁx)
(cid:73) Find all words that correspond to a given sequence of numbers
(cid:73) Database search, P2P network search, querying XML ﬁles
(cid:73) Computational biology
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
21 / 28

Suﬃx trees
A suﬃx tree for a string s is a compressed trie of the suﬃxes of s.
Example: s = abab; the set of suﬃxes: {$, b$, ab$, bab$, abab$}.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
23 / 28

Construction of a suﬃx tree
Add the longest suﬃx:
Add the suﬃx ab$:
Add the suﬃx bab$:
Add the suﬃx b$:
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
24 / 28

Construction of a suﬃx tree
Add the suﬃx $:
Label the leaf nodes (with the corresponding suﬃx index):
Time complexity: O(n2). Ukkonen’s algorithm: O(n).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
25 / 28

Application: string matching
Check if a substring (pattern) P(|P| = m) appears in a string T (|T | = n).
(cid:73) build a suﬃx tree for T (O(n))
(cid:73) cross the tree according to the P substring
(cid:73) each leaf in the identiﬁed subtree corresponds to an appearance (k
appearances)
Time complexity: O(n + k).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
26 / 28

Application: the longest common substring
A generalized suﬃx tree for a set of strings S is a compressed trie of the
suﬃxes s ∈ S.
Example: s1 = abab, s2 = aab; {$, b$, ab$, bab$, abab$},
{#, b#, ab#, aab#}.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
27 / 28

Application: the longest common substring
Example: s1 = abab, s2 = aab; {$, b$, ab$, bab$, abab$},
{#, b#, ab#, aab#}.
Any node that has $ and # in his subtree corresponds to a common
substring for s1 and s2. The longest common substring: the node that
satisﬁes the property and has the longest substring.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
28 / 28
